blocks.text: Prepared for {{fact:f:E-2007-001.client}} by Jason Jordan, Design Technician, Brackenridge Civil Group Inc.
Reporting period: since our last report.

The work is on schedule and within the fee. The design stands at about the midpoint. The condition survey is complete, the plan set is laid out and drawn as far as the survey supports, and the preliminary submittal is in preparation for issue at sixty percent. Nothing has occurred that changes the scope of the work, and we are not asking for anything from you at this time beyond the item noted under Risks.

The condition of the deck is close enough to what was assumed when the work was scoped that the design has not had to be reconsidered. That is the most useful thing in this report. On a rehabilitation the survey is where the schedule is usually lost, and it was not lost here.

items: Pamela Warren completed the condition survey of the deck, joints, railing, approach pavement, and drainage, and reconciled the findings against the record drawings. Delamination is confined to the two spans nearest the west abutment and to the shoulder areas rather than the wheel paths.
The sheet index was issued and the plan set laid out. A check set has been plotted and marked internally.
Kimberly Branch drew the standard details that could be taken from our existing library, and identified the two that require new work.

The traffic control approach was settled at a progress meeting: half-width construction under one-way alternating traffic, with a note permitting the contractor to propose a full closure of not more than one working day.
The specification outline was drafted and is with the Principal Engineer for review.

The record drawings furnished were reviewed sheet by sheet against the field measurements. Where the two disagree, the field measurement governs and the disagreement is noted on the sheet so that a reviewer can see it without having to ask.

blocks.text: A progress meeting was held at this office with your Director of Operations in attendance. Minutes were issued and are in the project file. The items raised there are carried in the design and none of them required a change to the scope.

items: Complete the sixty percent submittal and transmit it for your review and for the review of the agency having jurisdiction.
Prepare the preliminary construction cost estimate to accompany the submittal.
Draw the joint header and approach grading details.
Receive and answer review comments in writing, in the order they are issued, one response to each.
Advance the specification from outline to draft against the sixty percent sheets, so that the two are consistent before either is issued.
Begin the traffic control sheets, which follow from the half-width approach settled at the progress meeting.

blocks.text: We will telephone before the submittal is transmitted so that your review can be arranged in advance rather than around it. Pamela Warren will also be available to walk the set with your staff if that would be useful, and in our experience an hour spent that way returns more than a written comment does.

The engagement is a fixed fee of {{fact:f:E-2007-001.fee}}. The effort expended to date is in line with the work completed, and we do not foresee exceeding the fee for the scope as written. No additional services have been requested or performed. Should any be requested, we will price the work and obtain your written authorization before beginning it.

Record drawings. Two of the sheets furnished to us are illegible where they were faxed. If legible originals cannot be located, we will measure the joint and the header in the field. That costs us a day and it is not a change in scope, but it has to happen before the final details are drawn, so it is worth a look in your files.

Agency review. The length of the review of the preliminary submittal is not within our control. The schedule assumes a review of ordinary duration. A long review is the single item most likely to move the completion of the final plans, and it is the one we can do the least about.

Extent of repair. The survey establishes where the deck is sound and where it is not, but the true repair limits are not known until the contractor removes the wearing surface. The plans will handle this with unit price bid items for repair area rather than a lump sum, so that a surprise is priced in advance rather than negotiated after the fact.
Questions on any item in this report may be directed to Pamela Warren at this office.
